chunk_summary: This section outlines admission criteria for infants under 6 months with severe acute malnutrition (SAM), focusing on triage between inpatient care and community-based management (CMAM) pathways, as per WHO and national guidelines.
learning_objectives: Understand the admission criteria for infants under 6 months with SAM.
Differentiate between inpatient and CMAM pathways for managing SAM in infants.
Learn key guidelines from WHO and national protocols for SAM management.
Recognize the importance of early identification and treatment of SAM in infants.
key_facts: Infants under 6 months with SAM require specialized care due to their vulnerability and immature immune systems.
Admission criteria include visible signs of wasting, nutritional oedema, and failure to thrive.
WHO guidelines emphasize inpatient care for infants with medical complications or severe wasting.
Community-based management (CMAM) is recommended for stable cases without complications.
National protocols, such as those from India, align with WHO standards for facility-based care.
Early intervention improves survival rates and reduces long-term developmental impacts.
reference_quote: WHO guidelines recommend inpatient care for infants under 6 months with severe wasting or medical complications, while stable cases may be managed through CMAM pathways. (Source: https://www.who.int/tools/elena/interventions/sam-infants)
document_key_topics: Admission criteria for infants under 6 months with SAM
Triage between inpatient and CMAM pathways
WHO and national guidelines for SAM management
